perturbation.original_text: Section 3.1 ROYALTIES TO LICENSOR. (a) Licensee shall pay to Licensor for each Licensed Product licensed to a Redistributor or a Customer a royalty equal to the Specified Royalty Percentage of all revenues received (without deduction for value added tax, if any, but excluding any revenues for maintenance and support or upgrade services, which revenues are covered in paragraph (b) below) by Licensee under the Redistributor Agreement or Sublicense applicable to such Licensed Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3.1 ROYALTIES TO LICENSOR. (a) Licensee shall endeavor to pay to Licensor for each Licensed Product licensed to a Redistributor or a Customer a royalty equal to an appropriate percentage of revenues received (excluding certain revenues as outlined below) by Licensee under the Redistributor Agreement or Sublicense applicable to such Licensed Product. The determination of an appropriate percentage will be made at Licensee's sole discretion, considering market conditions.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "Specified Royalty Percentage" with "an appropriate percentage" and giving the Licensee sole discretion in determining the percentage based on "market conditions". This creates uncertainty and potentially contradicts the original intention of a fixed royalty rate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding implied duty of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware does not have a statute codifying the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts, Delaware case law has established that every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The modified text potentially violates this duty because granting the Licensee sole discretion to determine the royalty percentage allows them to act opportunistically and deprive the Licensor of the benefit of their bargain (a clearly defined royalty percentage). Such language might be interpreted as an attempt to avoid the original agreement's financial obligations, contradicting the implied duty to act in good faith and fairly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. 63 Del. Laws, c. 218, Â§ â1 ;Â 70 Del. Laws, c. 186, Â§ â1 ;Â 73 Del. Laws, c. 329, Â§ â33 ;Â Â§ 2002. Injunctive relief. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 63 Del. Laws, c. 218, Â§ â1 ;Â 71 Del. Laws, c. 80, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 2003. Damages. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriatorâs unauthorized disclosure or use
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language, by granting the Licensee sole discretion to determine the royalty percentage based on 'market conditions,' undermines the original agreement's defined royalty structure. This alteration introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially allowing the Licensee to act opportunistically and deprive the Licensor of the benefit of their bargain, specifically a clearly defined royalty percentage. The LLM Law Explanation indicates that this contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which Delaware case law recognizes. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses trade secrets and misappropriation, it does not directly apply to the core issue of contractual good faith. However, the absence of a specific snippet directly addressing the implied duty of good faith does not negate the argument that the modification creates a contradiction, as the Law Explanation provides a legal basis grounded in Delaware law (case law). The modification introduces ambiguity, creating a legal contradiction in the form of an Ambiguous Legal Obligation.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to any royalty payments due hereunder from Licensee to Licensor in excess of the amount of Royalty Advances made by Licensee from time to time hereunder, such payments shall be payable on the later of (i) sixty (60) days after the date of the applicable invoice to a Redistributor or Customer, as the case may be, or (ii) five (5) business days following Licensee's receipt of payment from a Redistributor or Customer, as the case may be.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to any royalty payments due hereunder from Licensee to Licensor beyond the Royalty Advances, payments will be made in a reasonable timeframe following receipt of payment from a Redistributor or Customer. The definition of a 'reasonable timeframe' will be determined based on internal accounting practices.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the specific payment terms (60 days after invoice or 5 business days after receipt) with "reasonable timeframe based on internal accounting practices," the obligation becomes vague and unenforceable. This introduces ambiguity that weakens the Licensor's right to timely payment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform Specified Obligations
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, a breach of contract occurs when a party fails to perform their obligations as outlined in the agreement. The altered language provides ambiguity around the payment term obligations, allowing the Licensee to manipulate payment schedules based on internal practices, potentially undermining the original agreement and the Licensor's right to timely payment. This could constitute a breach if the "reasonable timeframe" consistently delays payments beyond what was reasonably intended by the parties, frustrating the purpose of the contract.

perturbation.justification: The altered text replaces specific payment terms (60 days after invoice or 5 business days after receipt) with a vague "reasonable timeframe based on internal accounting practices." This introduces ambiguity that weakens the Licensor's right to timely payment and could allow the Licensee to manipulate payment schedules based on internal practices, potentially undermining the original agreement. The original text set a specific standard while the modified text removes the set standard and bases it on internal practices, which could be changed at any point by the Licensee. While neither snippet directly supports this assessment, the ambiguity of the changed text makes it more likely for a future violation to occur.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee may exercise the Option by written notice to Licensor and Skunkware at any time during the Option Period. Upon any such exercise of the Option, Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware (as the case may be) shall proceed to diligently and in good faith negotiate and execute a definitive purchase and sale agreement for Licensor's acquisition of all of the assets of, or outstanding capital stock in, Licensor, as the case may be.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee may consider exercising the Option by written notice to Licensor and Skunkware during the Option Period. Following such notice, Licensee and Licensor or Skunkware (as the case may be) will attempt to negotiate a definitive purchase and sale agreement for Licensor's acquisition. However, neither party is obligated to finalize or execute such an agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "Licensee may exercise the Option" to "Licensee may consider exercising the Option" and adding that "neither party is obligated to finalize or execute such an agreement," the original commitment to negotiate and execute a purchase agreement after the option is exercised becomes non-binding. This ambiguity significantly weakens the right granted to the Licensee through the Option to Purchase.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law regarding illusory promises in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, like other jurisdictions, recognizes that a contract must have mutuality of obligation. An illusory promise is a statement that appears to be a promise but does not actually obligate the promisor to do anything. The modification transforms the Licensee's obligation to negotiate and execute a purchase and sale agreement into a mere willingness to attempt negotiation, with no actual obligation to finalize the agreement. This lack of a binding commitment renders the Licensee's promise illusory, potentially making the entire option agreement unenforceable under Delaware contract law. Because the licensee now has no real obligation to do anything after exercising the option, there may be no mutual obligation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: illusory promise | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you illusory promise An illusory promise is a promise that is unenforceable due to indefiniteness or lack of mutuality , where only one side is bound to perform. The promise is normally a statement that seems to be an offer or agreement , but is so vague, ambiguous or conditional that it does not actually obligate the promisor to do anything at all. An example of this would be an agreement between a seller and buyer which states that the seller "agrees to sell all of the ice cream he wants to" to the buyer. An important in contract law that illustrates this concept is Hamer v. Sidway, 27 N.E. 256 (N.Y. 1891 ). In this case, an uncle promised his nephew that he would pay him $5,000 if the nephew refrained from drinking, using tobacco, swearing, or gambling until the age of 21. The nephew fulfilled his side of the bargain, but the uncle refused to pay, arguing that the promise was merely a gift and not a binding contract. The court held that the promise was not illusory because the nephew had given up a legal right (to engage in those activities) in exchange for the promise of payment. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an illusory promise, contradicting the requirement for mutuality of obligation in contract law. The original text requires diligent negotiation and execution of a purchase agreement, while the changed text makes this entirely optional, stating "neither party is obligated to finalize or execute such an agreement." This weakens the Licensee's right and creates ambiguity regarding the enforceability of the agreement, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines an 'illusory promise' as 'a promise that is unenforceable due to indefiniteness or lack of mutuality, where only one side is bound to perform,' and notes it is 'so vague, ambiguous or conditional that it does not actually obligate the promisor to do anything at all.' By removing the obligation to finalize the agreement, the modified text creates such an illusory promise.
